---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size40[

Explore the current state of human rights violations by states around the world.

]

<br>
<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Fall 2021)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Google doc with country sign-ups
- https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1aAYanwRF-6B5DsXQRr_uBDB6Wz7r5QktduIfqesBJZI/edit?usp=sharing

2. Data: Consider a few slides presenting data on their chosen cases to aid discussion of the sample

3. Spreadsheet with CIRIGHTS data for selected countries

<br>

COURSE EVALS DAY

- Grab direct link and email to class on Nov 29th





---

class: middle, slidegreen

.size50[.center[**For Monday after Thanksgiving**]]

.size35[Choose two countries: a democracy and a dictatorship (claim yours on Google Sheet, first-come, first-served). 

Review the current human rights record for both countries using all three sources below. 

1. The country reports by the US Department of State
2. The country reports by Amnesty International
3. The country reports by Human Rights Watch

Be ready to provide us an overview in class.
]

???

For today I asked each of you to perform two case studies that would help us think about the state of human rights in the world.

EVERYBODY READY TO GO?



---

class: middle, center, slidepurple

.size60[**A Case Study**]

.size40[
"A case study is an in-depth examination, often undertaken over time, of a single case – such as a policy, programme, intervention site, implementation process or participant" (Goodrick 2014, 1).
]

???

Your work for today is what we refer to as a "case study."

Although, to be fair to what we did, we did a very brief case study at a single moment in recent time...

Still, I think you get the gist.




---

class: middle, center, slidepurple

.size60[**A Comparative Case Study**]

.size40[
"Comparative case studies involve the analysis and synthesis of the similarities, differences and patterns across two or more cases that share a common focus or goal in a way that produces knowledge that is easier to generalize about causal questions – how and why particular programmes or policies work or fail to work" (Goodrick 2014).
]

???

Thanks to all of your work before class, we can now use our time in class to perform a comparative case analysis.

Gathering cases and then exploring them in a comparative fashion is an excellent method for:

- learning about the world,
- identifying puzzles you'd like to understand, and
- for developing new research projects or papers.

I'm hoping we can do a bit of all three of those things today.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/15_1-Sources.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

* NOTE TO YOU: Shorten discussion of bias in the sources *

Before we dig into the cases, let's talk about our data sources.

Heading into areas we know nothing about puts even more pressure on us to build knowledge using "high quality" sources.

SO, HOW DO WE USE THESE THREE SOURCES TO ESTABLISH THE HIGH CONFIDENCE FACTS?

* force discussion *

<br>

(Most confident where all three reports agree with each other?)
- This assumes all three asking the same question, right?

(Least confident when:)
- only one mentions something,
- when a "fact" lies at the heart of the source's bias,

<br>

TO WHAT DEGREE SHOULD WE ASSUME THAT THESE THREE SOURCES ARE INDEPENDENT FROM EACH OTHER?
- DOES EACH ADD SOMETHING NEW OR ARE THEY JUST PARROTING THE WORK OF THE OTHERS?

<br>

* Split into groups of four *

Try to work with new people!


---

class: middle, slidepurple

.size50[.center[**Compare and contrast the democracy cases**]]

.size30[
+ In general, how good is the respect for human rights in these cases?
	+ Excellent
	+ Very good
	+ Good
	+ Fair
	+ Poor

+ Most targeted groups?

+ Most violated rights?
]

???

* Report Back and Discuss *

OK, ANY EARLY CONCLUSIONS WE CAN DRAW AT THIS POINT ABOUT HUMAN RIGHTS PRACTICES IN DEMOCRACIES?

* DISCUSS *

- MOST COMMON "HUMAN RIGHTS" VIOLATIONS?

- WHO SPECIFICALLY IN THE COUNTRY IS DOING THE HARMS?
(Government? Rebels? Terrorists?)

- HOW ARE VICTIMS BEING TARGETED?
(how identified? race, class, ethnicity, immigration status)

- ANY EVIDENCE OF HARMS BEING ADDRESSED?




---

class: middle, slidepurple

.size50[.center[**Compare and contrast the dictatorship cases**]]

.size30[
+ In general, how good is the respect for human rights in these cases?
	+ Excellent
	+ Very good
	+ Good
	+ Fair
	+ Poor

+ Most targeted groups?

+ Most violated rights?
]

???

* Report Back and Discuss *

WHAT IS THE RECENT STATE OF HUMAN RIGHTS IN YOUR CHOSEN DICTATORSHIP?

Make clear for us which conclusions of yours are high confidence and which are lower confidence.



OK, ANY EARLY CONCLUSIONS WE CAN DRAW AT THIS POINT ABOUT HUMAN RIGHTS PRACTICES IN DICTATORSHIPS?

* DISCUSS *

- MOST COMMON "HUMAN RIGHTS" VIOLATIONS?

- WHO SPECIFICALLY IN THE COUNTRY IS DOING THE HARMS?
(Government? Rebels? Terrorists?)

- HOW ARE VICTIMS BEING TARGETED?
(how identified? race, class, ethnicity, immigration status)

- ANY EVIDENCE OF HARMS BEING ADDRESSED?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/15_1-human_rights_map.png')
background-size: 80%
background-position: center

???

Ok, that said, let's compare and contrast across our cases.

BASED ON THIS COMPARATIVE CASE STUDY HOW COMFORTABLE ARE YOU IN ASSERTING THAT REGIME TYPE IS A PREDICTOR OF HUMAN RIGHTS BEHAVIOR?

- WHY OR WHY NOT?

<br>

DO THE REGIME TYPES DIFFER SIGNIFICANTLY IN TERMS OF:

- MOST COMMON "HUMAN RIGHTS" VIOLATIONS?

- WHO SPECIFICALLY IN THE COUNTRY IS DOING THE HARMS?
(Government? Rebels? Terrorists?)

- HOW ARE VICTIMS BEING TARGETED?
(how identified? race, class, ethnicity, immigration status)

- ANY EVIDENCE OF HARMS BEING ADDRESSED?

<br>

Last big question for us today.

DO YOU THINK HUMAN RIGHTS VIOLATIONS SHOULD COMPEL AMERICAN ACTION? WHY OR WHY NOT?

- SHOULD WE BE THE WORLD'S POLICEMAN WHEN HUMAN RIGHTS ARE UNDER ASSAULT?


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/15_1-fragile_states.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Here we see the Fragile States Index.

If human rights violations are a prelude to state failure and state failure is a prelude to wider war then maybe we need to be more proactive on this?

YOU BUY THAT? WHY OR WHY NOT?

<br>

On Wednesday we'll dig into a research article that aims to measure if these kinds of reports make peoples' lives better or if they don't matter....
